title: Savings Rate Rule
content: Save about 30% of your take-home pay each month before other expenses. This amount should be invested or saved to meet your financial goals[1].

title: Budget Rule (50-30-20)
content: Use the 50/30/20 budgeting rule: allocate 50% of income to essentials, 30% to wants/discretionary expenses, and 20% to savings[2].

title: Emergency Fund Rule
content: Keep an emergency fund of about 12 months of essential expenses (including existing EMIs) in liquid form. This covers sudden income loss or emergencies[3].

title: Emergency Fund Storage
content: Park your emergency savings in liquid instruments (savings account or liquid mutual fund) separate from your regular investments for quick access[4].

title: Life Insurance Rule
content: Maintain term life insurance coverage of 10–15 times your annual income to protect dependents in case of unexpected death[5].

title: House Affordability
content: Ensure the value of a home does not exceed 2–3 times your annual income when taking a home loan. This keeps EMIs manageable[6].

title: Car Affordability
content: Keep a car’s on-road price under ~50% of your annual income. Follow the 20-4-10 rule: 20% down payment, ≤4-year loan, and EMI ≤10% of income[7].

title: Education Loan Rule
content: Take an education loan only up to your expected first-year salary (or current salary) to avoid EMIs that are unaffordable after graduation[8].

title: EMI (Debt) Rule
content: Ensure total EMIs (all loan payments) do not exceed about 40% of your net income. Borrow only as much as you can comfortably repay[9].

title: Retirement Corpus Rule
content: Aim for a retirement corpus ~20× your final annual salary. In retirement, you should generate ~80% of your pre-retirement income from your savings/investments[10][11].

title: SIP Investing Rule
content: Invest regularly via SIPs to harness compounding. The key is to “start early and invest regularly” for long-term wealth growth[12].

title: Age-Based Equity Rule
content: Allocate roughly (100 – your age)% of your portfolio to equity, with the rest in safer assets. For example, a 30-year-old might allocate ~70% to equities[13].

title: Diversification Rule
content: Spread investments across at least three asset classes (e.g. equity, debt, gold), with at least 10% in each, to reduce risk[14].

title: Investment vs Saving Rule
content: Once your emergency fund is in place, invest surplus money instead of keeping it idle. Market-linked investments (like mutual funds) can outpace inflation, while plain saving mostly preserves capital[15].

title: Section 80C Deduction
content: Under Section 80C, invest up to ₹1.5 lakh per year in eligible instruments (PPF, ELSS, life insurance, etc.) to claim that amount as a tax deduction[16].

title: NPS Deduction (80CCD1B)
content: Contribute to the National Pension System (NPS) and claim an additional ₹50,000 deduction under Section 80CCD(1B), over and above the ₹1.5 lakh limit of Sec.80C[17].

title: Health Insurance Deduction (80D)
content: Claim up to ₹25,000 deduction on health insurance premiums under Sec.80D (₹50,000 if covering senior citizen parents)[18].

title: Education Loan Deduction (80E)
content: Claim 100% of interest paid on a higher-education loan (no maximum limit) as a deduction for up to 8 years under Sec.80E[19].

title: First-Home Loan Deduction (80EE)
content: First-time homebuyers (loan in FY2016-17) can claim an extra ₹50,000 deduction on home loan interest under Sec.80EE (subject to property/loan caps)[20].

title: NPS Contribution Deduction (80CCD1)
content: Under Sec.80CCD(1), employees get deduction on NPS contributions up to 10% of salary. Employer NPS contribution up to 14% is deductible under 80CCD(2), not counted in the ₹2 lakh limit[21].

title: Interest Income Deduction (80TTA)
content: Under Section 80TTA, you can claim up to ₹10,000 per year in deduction on interest earned from savings bank accounts[22].

title: Tax Regime Choice
content: Many deductions (80C, 80CCD(1B), 80D, etc.) apply only under the old tax regime. Compare your tax under old vs new regimes before filing to ensure maximum benefits[18][23].
